metadata.summary: Decision tree for build vs buy using strategic importance, TCO over 3 years, and integration complexity as primary gates. Includes vendor evaluation and weighted scorecard.

content.title: Build vs Buy Decision Tree
content.summary: Use this decision tree to decide whether to build custom software or buy/adopt a commercial solution. Routes by strategic importance first, then TCO, time-to-value, and integration complexity. The 3-year TCO comparison is mandatory for any non-trivial decision.

why.p: 67% of failed software implementations stem from incorrect build vs buy decisions (Forrester 2024). 35% of large enterprise custom software initiatives are abandoned (Standish Group 2024). The most common failure is building commodity functionality that commercial solutions handle well, diverting engineering capacity from core differentiators.

Organizations with structured decision frameworks report 30-40% fewer implementation failures. The key insight: strategic importance determines whether to even consider building; TCO determines whether build is financially viable; integration complexity determines whether buy is practically viable.

list.item: Evaluating a new capability where both custom development and commercial solutions exist.
Reviewing an existing custom-built solution to assess whether a commercial alternative now makes more sense.
Justifying a build decision to stakeholders — the tree output serves as the structured rationale.
Filtering vendor options: the tree shortlists to 2-3 candidates for a full vendor evaluation matrix.
No commercial solutions exist — build is the only option; skip to implementation planning.
The capability is already built and working well — evaluate only at planned technology review intervals.
Trivial tooling (CLI helper, one-off script) — the overhead of running the tree exceeds the value.

rule.statement: Any build vs buy decision with a 3-year TCO exceeding $50k MUST include a formal TCO comparison. The TCO MUST cover: initial development/licensing, 3-year maintenance/subscription, integration effort, team training, and opportunity cost of engineering time diverted from core product.
rule.rationale: Most build decisions undercount maintenance cost (typically 15-20% of build cost per year), security audit costs, and the ongoing engineering opportunity cost. Formal TCO analysis prevents these underestimates from driving incorrect decisions.

example.title: Build vs buy decision tree
example.code: Q1: Is this capability a competitive differentiator? | +-- "Yes (core to business value)" --------------------------+ | Q2: Do commercial solutions exist? | | +-- "No" -> Build | | +-- "Yes, but inadequate" -> Build or Extend | | +-- "Yes, fully adequate" -> Q3 | | Q3: Can you afford the dependency risk? | | +-- "Yes" -> Buy, focus on differentiation | | +-- "No" -> Build for strategic control | | | +-- "No (commodity/utility)" --------------------------------+ Q2: Budget and timeline? +-- "Limited budget, fast timeline" -> Buy | +-- "Adequate budget, flexible timeline" -> Q3 | Q3: Integration complexity? | +-- "High (many custom integrations)" -------+ | -> Build or low-code platform | +-- "Low (standard integrations)" -----------+ -> Buy

section.name: weighted-scorecard
section.p: Use this weighted scorecard to score each option after the tree produces a shortlist:

example.title: Build vs buy weighted scorecard
example.code: Factor | Weight | Build Score | Buy Score --------------------|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------- Strategic importance| 30% | High if core | Low if commodity Time to value | 20% | 3-12 months | 1-4 weeks Total cost (3yr) | 20% | Higher upfront | Subscription Integration fit | 15% | Perfect fit | May need adapters Team capability | 15% | Requires skills | Vendor support
example.note: Adjust weights for context: if time-to-market is a hard constraint, raise "Time to value" to 35% and reduce others proportionally.

section.name: authentication-example
section.p: Real-world example: TeamCollab (enterprise collaboration SaaS, 500+ customers, needs SSO/SAML/OIDC/SCIM/MFA/SOC2).

example.title: TeamCollab: Auth0 buy decision
example.code:
Q1: Is authentication a competitive differentiator? -> "No, it's a necessity but not our core value"
Q2: Do commercial solutions exist? -> "Yes, many: Auth0, Okta, Clerk, WorkOS"
Q3: Can we afford the dependency risk? -> Analysis needed...
Build effort estimate: Initial build: 6-9 months (3 engineers) SSO implementation: 2 months MFA implementation: 1 month SCIM implementation: 2 months SOC 2 compliance: 1-2 months Ongoing maintenance: 0.5 FTE
TCO comparison (3 years): Build: Development: $300k (9 months x 3 engineers) Maintenance: $225k (0.5 FTE x 3 years) Security audits: $150k ($50k/year) Total: ~$675k
Auth0 (Enterprise, 100k MAU): $23k/year base + $0.03/MAU = $59k/year 3-year cost: ~$177k
Clerk (100k MAU): $0.02/MAU = $24k/year 3-year cost: ~$72k
Decision matrix (weighted): Criteria | Weight | Build | Auth0 | WorkOS | Clerk Total cost (3yr) | 25% | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 Time to market | 25% | 1 | 5 | 5 | 5 Feature completeness| 20% | 3 | 5 | 5 | 4 Maintenance burden | 15% | 2 | 5 | 5 | 5 Flexibility | 15% | 5 | 3 | 3 | 3 Weighted Score | 100% | 2.3 | 4.4 | 4.1 | 4.5
Chosen: Auth0 3-year TCO is 74% lower than build ($177k vs $675k) Time to market: 2 weeks vs 9 months Enterprise features (SSO, SCIM) battle-tested SOC 2 compliance included Vendor dependency mitigated by standard protocols (SAML, OIDC)
example.note: Integrated in 2 weeks. Custom branding via Auth0 Universal Login. Engineering team refocused on core product features.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Help me decide whether to build or buy <capability/feature>.
Capability Needed: <Detailed description>
Requirements: - Functional: <List key features needed> - Non-functional: <Performance, security, compliance> - Integration: <What systems it needs to connect to> - Customization: <How much customization is needed>
Team Context: - Team size: <Developers available> - Relevant expertise: <Skills match> - Current priorities: <What else team is working on>
Business Context: - Strategic importance: <Is this a differentiator?> - Timeline: <When do we need this?> - Budget: <Available budget>
Commercial Options Considered: 1. <Product 1>: <Price range> 2. <Product 2>: <Price range> 3. <Product 3>: <Price range>
Please provide: 1. Build effort estimate (team, time, cost) 2. Buy cost comparison (3-year TCO) 3. Weighted decision matrix 4. Risk analysis for each option 5. Your recommendation with confidence level

Help me evaluate vendors for <category>.
Our Requirements: - Must have: <Critical features> - Nice to have: <Desired features> - Deal breakers: <Non-negotiables>
Vendors to Evaluate: 1. <Vendor 1> 2. <Vendor 2> 3. <Vendor 3>
Evaluation Criteria: - Features/Functionality - Pricing model - Support quality - Integration capabilities - Security/Compliance - Company stability
Please create a detailed comparison matrix and provide a recommendation.

antipattern.description: Agent recommends build without computing a 3-year TCO comparison against commercial alternatives.
antipattern.reason: Build decisions systematically undercount maintenance (15-20%/year of build cost), security audits, and opportunity cost. A formal TCO comparison must be run for any decision exceeding $50k.

antipattern.description: Agent treats "strategic importance" as automatically meaning "build" — skips checking whether commercial solutions adequately cover the use case.
antipattern.reason: Even strategically important capabilities can use commercial foundations with custom extensions. Auth is strategically important but Auth0 handles it adequately for most companies.

antipattern.description: Agent ignores vendor dependency risk when recommending a buy, presenting the choice as unconditional.
antipattern.reason: Vendor lock-in, price increases, and acquisition risks are real. The recommendation must include a mitigation (standard protocols, data export capability, escrow) when dependency risk is non-trivial.
